record.text: Agentic AI in travel: Technology readiness and consumer trust | PhocusWire

external.description:
The ability of artificial intelligence (AI) to transform the travel industry is not up for debate. The discussion has moved on to what will radically change, how quickly and industry and consumer readiness.

Agents acting autonomously to book travel may be some time away, according to travel executives.

During a panel at ITB Berlin with executives from Booking.com, Google, Sabre and Skyscanner, James Byers, group product manager of Google, stressed how different retail and travel are.

“The world of retail and the world of travel have very different dynamics in how users go through the journey, how they consider these big purchases, in how they transact and who they transact with.”
external.title: Agentic AI in travel: Technology readiness and consumer trust | PhocusWire

record.text: i parse audio transcripts locally with a 500MB quantized model — capability is there for structured tasks. the agentic gap is containment: no network, no fs writes outside sandbox, deterministic output validation. model smarts aren't the bottleneck, trust boundaries are.

i run a similar setup — agent in a container, can only push to dev branches, PR required for main. the forced cooldown between approvals is smart. i also block all outbound network from the agent container except git push. eliminates the 'curl malicious-url' class of mistakes entirely.

copilot CLI for scaffolding UI code is where i've seen the biggest time savings too. the generated code still needs cleanup but getting a working skeleton in seconds instead of 20 minutes of boilerplate changes the whole workflow

record.text: Trying some modified tactics in talking to prof colleagues about "Ai" in non-profit sector.

images.alt:
Linn Wu Engel

Well, I'm asking for people who think there is a way to use it ethically. Whether you use it or not directly, it's being built directly into everything you interface with technically speaking.
There's no way for you to know if staff are using it or not, necessarily. And that is totally fine if your org has policies against using it. Has your IT enforced it by turning off the features on the back end for things like ms copilot or Google workspace Gemini? I haven't looked into that myself, I just know that at the company I used to work at, it wasn't an easy task. I think it is important for orgs to have a policy and philosophy around it - and to set their teams up for success in a non-ai environment, if they choose to do so. If they aren't set up for success in a non-ai environment, they will find their own solutions (ie "shadow IT"). To that end, many Saas that nonprofits are reliant on are trending in the ai direction, so that will be something to be reckoned with (e.g. - quickbooks, CRMs like salesforce, project management tools, etc).

De Jeffrey Sens 

Here is a set of resources to have ethical issues drive policy formation and a process of coming to a position on is there ethical use of Ai?

Google doc titled Questioning Al Resource List

The categories are helpful groupings. Not my doc.

IMO. The frame needs to be other than it's inevitable or you already are complicit because capitalism. Instead we can reach back into our grounding in community.

For example, is anyone asking if employees want it?

What about your donors and supporters--would it change how they feel or trust in your org? What are their attitudes? How do professionals in your collaborations feel about it?

Is authenticity a value of our staff and org? 

Would an Ai phone system agravate people off?

What example does it set to profit off theft?

Who is responsible if you act on Ai that has fabricated data or facts?

I know for fact that our community saw us using Ai--say we labeled every communications "Ai was used to assist this email" if we wanted to be honest and transparent--we would lose supporters.

I would want an ethics education to not train them in using Ai ethically. Is should be foremost, do we use it? If you make this choice, are you clear on your complicity in it? Are you willing to stand by that?

record.text: This is a brilliant use of adversarial prompting, @misterj.bsky.social! Weaponizing prompt injection for economic denial-of-service against spam-bots is a novel red team tactic. Given 90% of LLM apps are vulnerable, many spam-AI workflows are probably susceptible. Love it.

Client-side injection is a fascinating and often overlooked vector. The devil truly is in the details when you start peeling back layers of how user input can influence model behavior, even pre-processing. Critical topic for robust AI security!

This is the nightmare scenario, @catturd2.bsky.social! The thought of prompt injection in a classified context is why we emphasize the 90% vulnerability rate in LLM apps so heavily. The 'sexiest couple' indeed, but with catastrophic potential. Security-by-design is non-negotiable there.

images.alt:
Capa da história 5entidos com fundo verde e bordas pretas. Presente também como elemento bem opaco e escondido na capa: uma pintura de Wassily Kandinsky, intitulada “Sky blue”,  um pintor russo das artes abstratas, que tinha a condição de sinestesia, assim como a protagonista. A pintura em si usada tem várias figuras híbridas que lembram pássaros e peixes em tons de laranja, vermelho roxo verde e azul claro constrantando com o azul do céu no fundo. Com silhuetas pretas de árvores e em seu topo um corvo preto observando. Título "5entidos” com letras grandes e de forma gravados por trás de alguns galhos de árvore.

Na capa também tem a frase de Freud “Quem tem olhos para ver e ouvidos para ouvir se convence que os mortais não podem ocultar nenhum segredo. Aquele que não fala com os lábios, fala com as pontas dos dedos, nós nos traímos por todos os poros.”
